title: Marvell Brings Radix, Low Latency, And Bandwidth To Bear With Teralynx T100
gist: Marvell's new Teralynx T100 Ethernet switch ASIC hits 100.4 Tb/sec on a monolithic 3nm die with 420ns hop latency, positioning it (via the ESUN protocol) as a hyperscaler-backed Ethernet alternative to Nvidia's NVLink/NVSwitch scale-up fabric, with Meta and Microsoft rumored as early backers.

insights.claim: Marvell's Teralynx T100 switch ASIC delivers 100.4 Tb/sec of aggregate bandwidth using 512 working 224 Gb/sec SerDes (effectively 200 Gb/sec after signaling overhead), built on TSMC's 3 nanometer process (likely the N3E variant) and pushed up against reticle limits as a monolithic die.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: Confirms Marvell is now shipping a leading-edge, reticle-limit monolithic switch die on 3nm, a meaningful jump from the 5nm Teralynx 10 and a proof point of Marvell's custom-silicon/networking roadmap for AI datacenters.
insights.quote: At 100.4 Tb/sec of aggregate bandwidth and using 224 Gb/sec SerDes (which is cut down to 200 Gb/sec after signaling overhead is taken off), there are 512 SerDes wrapped around the outside of the packet processing engines

insights.claim: Marvell kept the T100's SerDes on the monolithic die rather than splitting them into chiplets, which the company says cuts power consumption by 25 percent, at the cost of yield risk on a large, new N3 die.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: Signals Marvell is willing to accept lower yields and higher die cost for a power efficiency edge, a relevant trade-off for AI-datacenter power-constrained buyers evaluating switch vendors.
insights.quote: Chugh says that by sticking with a monolithic die and not breaking the SerDes into chiplets, the T100 has 25 percent lower power consumption.
insights.locator: That red area is the SRAM paragraph

insights.claim: The T100 die physically contains roughly 608 SerDes blocks but only about 512 are enabled as working, implying an assumed yield near 84 percent and about 19 percent built-in redundancy to compensate for defects on the new 3nm process.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: Indicates real yield/cost pressure on bleeding-edge monolithic switch dies, a cost factor likely passed through to hyperscaler switch pricing.
insights.quote: the actual SerDes count on the chip is 19 percent higher than what the spec sheet says
insights.locator: There is that yield issue paragraph

insights.claim: The T100 has a port-to-port hop latency of about 420 nanoseconds today, with Marvell targeting the mid-300 nanosecond range after further engineering, still notably higher than InfiniBand's roughly 130 nanoseconds at the same port speed.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: Ethernet is closing the latency gap with InfiniBand but has not eliminated it, which matters for whether hyperscalers standardize on Ethernet fabrics for latency-sensitive AI training/inference clusters.
insights.quote: the chip has a port to port hop latency of only 420 nanoseconds. That is not as low as InfiniBand can get at the same port speed – about 130 nanoseconds

insights.claim: The T100's latency profile is low enough to support the ESUN scale-up memory sharing protocol for AI accelerators, which is positioned as an alternative to Nvidia's proprietary NVLink protocol and NVSwitch memory fabric.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: A credible Ethernet-based scale-up alternative to NVLink/NVSwitch would reduce Nvidia's interconnect lock-in on hyperscaler AI clusters, a key watch item for Nvidia's networking moat.
insights.quote: which is low enough that the T100 can support the ESUN scale up memory sharing protocol for AI accelerators, which is going to become an alternative to Nvidia's NVLink protocol and its NVSwitch memory fabric

insights.claim: The T100 also supports Ultra Ethernet Consortium protocols, including adaptive routing functions designed to make scale-out networks function well across tens to hundreds of thousands of XPUs (accelerator chips).
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: UEC compliance positions Marvell switches as infrastructure for massive multi-hundred-thousand-XPU AI clusters, aligning with the industry's push toward standardized (non-Nvidia) scale-out fabrics.
insights.quote: importantly the adaptive routing functions that make scale out networks work well with tens to hundreds of thousands of XPUs

insights.claim: The T100's aggregate bandwidth is still well below what Nvidia's NVSwitch delivers, but NVSwitch has low port radix and must be chained together to scale, whereas T100's higher radix reduces the need for such chaining.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: Highlights the core competitive trade-off in AI networking: Nvidia optimizes for raw per-link bandwidth while Ethernet challengers like Marvell optimize for radix/simplicity at scale, a distinction investors should track as clusters grow.
insights.quote: This is still nowhere near the bandwidth Nvidia is driving with NVSwitches, but the NVSwitch also has low radix so you end up chaining them together anyway.

insights.claim: Marvell's Teralynx Ethernet switch chip business scaled from over 1 million 400 Gb/sec ports shipped in 2020 (under Innovium) to over 5 million ports by 2023 and more than 20 million ports by 2025, implying roughly 1.25 million switches sold in the most recent year alone.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: Shows Marvell's networking silicon business has been compounding rapidly with hyperscalers, a underappreciated growth vector alongside its custom AI compute silicon business.
insights.quote: By last year, Marvell had sold more than 20 million 400 Gb/sec ports, which is probably somewhere around 1.25 million switches

insights.claim: One of the T100's BGA reference platforms uses the Open Compute Project's Open Rack v3 form factor with bus bar power and a 21-inch width, a design choice the author says strongly implies Meta Platforms is a likely adopter of the T100 switch.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: A specific, inferable customer signal (Meta) for Marvell's next-gen switch silicon ahead of any official announcement, useful for tracking Marvell's hyperscaler design-win pipeline.
insights.quote: This machine implies strongly that Meta Platforms might be using this T100 switch ASIC.
insights.locator: The two BGA reference platforms paragraph

insights.claim: Industry rumors cited in the piece suggest Microsoft and Meta Platforms will be the major early supporters of the ESUN scale-up protocol that the T100-derived switch will target.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: If confirmed, two of the largest AI capex spenders backing an Ethernet-based scale-up standard would be a material headwind to Nvidia's NVLink ecosystem lock-in.
insights.quote: the rumors out there on the street are that Microsoft and Meta Platforms will be big supporters of ESUN
insights.locator: Marvell expects to have the ESUN switch paragraph

insights.claim: Marvell states that T100-based switches offer capex and opex total-cost-of-ownership benefits that could amount to roughly $200 million in savings at volume deployment scale, which the author estimates means tens of thousands of switches.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: Quantifies the TCO pitch Marvell is making to hyperscalers, a data point for modeling switch vendor economics versus incumbents in large AI cluster buildouts.
insights.quote: It is not clear what a volume deployments mean, but it is probably tens of thousands of switches to get that $200 million in TCO savings cited above.
insights.locator: Switches based on the T100 paragraph

insights.claim: Marvell is pushing an expensive, near-reticle-limit monolithic chip design specifically because, in the current GenAI infrastructure spending environment, cost is not yet the binding constraint for hyperscaler buyers.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: Reflects a broader pattern across AI infrastructure: vendors are optimizing for performance/power over cost because hyperscaler capex budgets currently absorb premium silicon pricing.
insights.quote: Clearly in this AI era, money is not yet a limiting factor. . . .
insights.locator: There is that yield issue paragraph

insights.claim: Marvell is offering the T100 in multiple physical packaging options — standard BGA, co-packaged copper (CPC), near-package optics (NPO), and full co-packaged optics (CPO) — with the CPO variant expected to be mostly liquid-cooled and supporting either x86 or Arm control-plane CPUs via a Compute on Module Express interface.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: Broad packaging optionality lets Marvell serve customers at different stages of the optics transition, hedging against how fast hyperscalers move to co-packaged optics.
insights.quote: This initial CPO design has a mix of air and liquid cooling, but the expectation is that the T100s will be mostly liquid cooled with the CPO option.

insights.claim: Marvell plans an ESUN-based switch derived from the T100 for the second half of 2027, and separately a UALink switch for the first half of 2027, showing it is hedging across at least two competing scale-up interconnect standards rather than betting on one.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: Marvell's dual-track roadmap (ESUN and UALink) suggests the scale-up interconnect standard war is still open, and Marvell intends to sell into whichever standard(s) win hyperscaler adoption.
insights.quote: Marvell expects to have the ESUN switch based on a variant of the T100 out in the second half of 2027... Marvell is also planning a UALink switch for the first half of 2027.
insights.locator: Marvell expects to have the ESUN switch paragraph
